old_claim.note: Invention Privilege Systems is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim_absent: No direct Technical Drawing Standards to Invention Privilege Systems edge remains because the source-backed node is scoped to machine-book illustration conventions, not patent privilege administration.

source_shape.source_locator: Collection note frames Ramelli's 1588 book as a late-sixteenth-century theatre of machines with illustrated engineering designs and detailed bilingual descriptions.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports technical machine illustrations as a publishing and engineering-communication practice independent of patent examination or privilege systems.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Patent systems may later use drawings, but Ramelli's machine-book evidence establishes the scoped technical drawing convention without requiring patent institutions as a predecessor.

ontology_before: Technical Drawing Standards depended directly on early patent systems, conflating invention-privilege administration with technical illustration practice.
ontology_after: Technical Drawing Standards are scoped to late Renaissance machine-book illustration conventions; patent workflows remain downstream or adjacent uses.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: removed the direct early_patent_systems prerequisite. Preserved: downstream patent specification and examination nodes may still depend on technical drawings.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to patent drawings only.
A source shows Renaissance technical machine drawing conventions emerged specifically from patent privilege administration.
A separate patent_drawing_requirements node is introduced and intentionally depends on early patent systems.
short_reason: Patent administration is an application context, not a prerequisite for the Ramelli-style technical drawing scope.
